sectors.name: Utilities
sectors.reason: Gas and oil generation costs surge. Regulated utilities cannot pass all costs through.

signals.label: Gold
signals.netSummary: Positive for gold holders. Geopolitical risk premium adds to existing Iran floor.
goodFor: Gold holders — existing positions benefit from geopolitical bid
Gold miners — operating leverage amplifies earnings
Portfolio hedgers — gold reduces correlation risk in a sell-off
badFor: Short gold traders — caught on wrong side of geopolitical spike
USD strength creates mild headwind — dollar and gold typically move inversely
signals.changeSignal: Gold bid fades if diplomatic resolution reduces geopolitical uncertainty
signals.explanation: Gold is a geopolitical hedge with no counterparty risk. In a scenario where US-Iran conflict could escalate unpredictably, investors want an asset that holds value regardless of which institutions or currencies are stressed.

consequences.then: Gold miners see earnings and equity re-rating simultaneously
JPY, CHF, Treasuries also bid
Inflation hedge narrative strengthens — more institutional allocation
Gold spikes quickly from elevated base

sectors.name: Gold Miners
sectors.reason: Gold above $2,900 re-rates miners strongly. High operating leverage to gold price.

sectors.name: Jewellery Retail
sectors.reason: Higher gold input costs squeeze margins unless passed to consumers.

sectors.name: Central Banks
sectors.reason: Many are net buyers of gold as reserve diversification. Higher price = paper gain.

sectors.name: Commodity Funds
sectors.reason: Gold weighting performs. Broad commodity indices benefit from oil and gold together.

stories.mechanism: The Strait of Hormuz carries approximately 20% of global oil supply. A credible closure threat — even without actual closure — is sufficient to reprice energy markets upward immediately. That oil price rise feeds into headline CPI within 6–8 weeks via energy bills, transport costs, and manufacturing inputs. At that point the Federal Reserve faces the monetary policy dilemma: inflation is rising and growth is slowing simultaneously. Raising rates fights inflation but accelerates the slowdown. Cutting rates cushions growth but entrenches the inflation. There is no clean move.
stories.pullquote: The threat is the shock. The price move is not irrational — it is the market doing exactly what markets are supposed to do.

bankingProducts.detail: This is the primary desk event. Energy derivatives across the curve reprice immediately. Brent prompt spreads widen on supply uncertainty. Middle distillate cracks — jet fuel, gasoil — spike on route disruption risk. Oil options vol surges particularly in near-dated contracts. Tanker freight derivatives move sharply as alternative route capacity is priced in. Any client with physical oil exposure or unhedged energy cost lines needs immediate attention.

The Fed's cutting path moves immediately. OIS strip reprices — June and September cut probabilities collapse as oil inflation feeds into the forward CPI path. 10yr Treasury yields rise as term premium expands on geopolitical uncertainty. The curve steepens: short-end rates stay anchored by Fed hold, long-end rises on inflation concern. Swaption vol across the curve picks up significantly.

USD strengthens broadly as safe haven flows dominate. Oil-exporting EM currencies — Russian rouble, Saudi riyal, Norwegian krone — benefit. Oil-importing EM currencies — Indian rupee, Turkish lira, South African rand — face pressure from both higher import costs and dollar strengthening. JPY and CHF bid on risk-off. EUR/USD faces the double pressure of USD strength and eurozone energy cost concerns.

Investment grade spreads widen modestly — risk-off sentiment. High yield spreads widen more significantly, particularly energy importers and consumer-facing issuers exposed to higher input costs. Airlines and logistics sector bonds come under immediate pressure. Energy sector bonds benefit — higher oil = stronger cash flows for producers. Sovereign spreads in oil-importing EM nations widen on fiscal concern.

Sector rotation is immediate and mechanical. Energy stocks — BP, Shell, TotalEnergies, Schlumberger — rally sharply. Defence stocks — BAE Systems, Raytheon, Thales — move higher on geopolitical spending expectations. Airlines sell off on fuel cost exposure. Consumer discretionary under pressure. High-multiple tech faces the discount rate mechanism. Equity vol — VIX — rises but remains below fear threshold at 18. Options market makers adjust delta hedges across the board.

Letters of credit and documentary credits for any trade passing through or near the Strait of Hormuz face elevated risk assessment. Marine insurance premiums for vessels transiting the Gulf spike immediately — war risk clauses activate. Trade finance desks need to review facility utilisation for clients with Middle East supply chains. Commodity finance — particularly oil-backed lending — faces collateral value volatility.

items.means: Threat becomes operational — oil moves to $105+, risk premium becomes a supply shock

items.threshold: Any Gulf state intermediary publicly activating
items.means: De-escalation pathway open — risk premium begins draining, partial oil reversal
Partial offset to supply fear — oil partially reverses, some risk premium drains

items.threshold: Second carrier group deployed to Gulf
items.means: Escalation signal — markets price longer disruption, oil holds gains
Oil shock officially in the inflation data — Fed cuts pushed to December or beyond

items.threshold: War risk premium above 1% of vessel value
items.means: Physical market disruption beginning — shipping costs cascade into goods inflation

items.threshold: Suez and Cape routes at capacity
items.means: Hormuz effectively closed — prolonged supply shock, 6–18 month elevated energy prices
Resolution pathway emerging — oil begins to price normalisation 3–6 months out

history.event: Arab oil embargo
history.what: Price quadrupled in six months. Took three years to normalise. Triggered recession, double-digit inflation, and forced aggressive Fed rate hikes.

history.event: Gulf War
history.what: Oil spiked 90% in three months then collapsed once coalition response was clear. Fast in, fast out — because military resolution was swift.

history.event: Russia/Ukraine
history.what: Brent went from $80 to $130 in three weeks. Took fourteen months to return to pre-invasion levels. Fed cited energy inflation as reason for aggressive hiking.

stories.lesson: Politically driven oil shocks resolve slower than logistical ones. 1990 reversed in 6 months once military resolution was clear. 1973 took 3 years because it was a political instrument. The current scenario is political — history suggests 6–18 months of elevated energy prices.

theories.name: Supply Shock Theory
theories.body: An exogenous event suddenly reduces the supply of a key input. Because oil is an input into almost everything, the price rise cascades through the entire economy. Qualitatively different from demand-driven inflation and significantly harder for central banks to address.

theories.name: Risk Premium Theory
theories.body: Markets price probability, not certainty. The mere credibility of a Hormuz closure is sufficient to add a risk premium to every barrel of oil traded globally. This premium can be larger than the eventual supply disruption — markets overshoot in uncertainty and partially reverse when tail risk is removed.

theories.name: Monetary Policy Dilemma
theories.body: Central banks normally manage the trade-off between inflation and unemployment. A supply shock breaks this mechanism — it generates high inflation and rising unemployment simultaneously. The Fed's tools work against each other. This is the defining feature of stagflation.

timeline.text: Oil spikes on risk premium. Bond yields rise. Growth equities sell off. Gold bids as geopolitical hedge.
Critical window. IRGC movements and diplomatic signals determine whether this is spike-and-fade or structural.
Higher oil feeds into CPI. Inflation expectations reprice upward. Rate cut probability falls further.
If de-escalation: oil partially reverses, risk premium drains, growth equities recover sharply. If escalation holds: stagflation dynamics entrench.
Political resolution or prolonged elevated energy prices. Historical base case for political shocks: 12–18 months before normalisation.

investor.title: If you manage investments
points: Rotate toward energy — BP, Shell, TotalEnergies all benefit directly. If the shock is political it could last 12–18 months.
Reduce high-multiple growth equity exposure — the discount rate mechanism works against these while yields stay elevated.
Consider gold as a portfolio hedge — at $2,840 geopolitical risk could push it meaningfully higher.
Defence sector is already moving — the structural case for higher defence spending extends well beyond this event.

professional.title: If you work in finance
points: Energy cost assumptions in any financial model need updating immediately — $94 oil with upside risk changes break-even calculations.
For deal teams: energy sector M&A picks up in supply shock environments — distressed assets in downstream and midstream become interesting.
For risk managers: stress-test against $120+ oil — what does that do to your portfolio?
For FP&A: any business with significant logistics or transport cost exposure needs a fuel cost scenario in the 2026 forecast now.

student.title: If you are studying economics
points: Real-time demonstration of why supply shocks are harder to manage than demand shocks — the central bank's standard toolkit does not apply cleanly.
Watch Risk Premium Theory operate: oil prices moved before any physical disruption. The market is pricing a probability distribution.
The Monetary Policy Dilemma is playing out live — follow how the Fed communicates its response over coming weeks.
Compare to the 1973 oil shock — the mechanism is identical, the scale and context are different. Worth an essay.

keyDates.note: First 48–72 hours critical. Any Iranian naval assets moving toward Hormuz changes this from threat to supply shock.
How tight is the supply buffer? A low inventory reading amplifies the oil spike.
If oil feeds into this reading faster than expected, Fed rate cut probability collapses entirely.
OPEC may increase production to offset Hormuz risk — would partially reverse the oil spike.

connectedStories.headline: ECB holds rates despite rising inflation.
connectedStories.connection: Oil above $90 feeds import inflation into the eurozone — tightening the ECB's policy dilemma and reducing the chance of the summer cut markets were hoping for.
connectedStories.globalScene: The Iran strike has effectively made the ECB's job harder without the ECB doing anything. This is how geopolitical shocks transmit through the global financial system — not through direct channels but through the inflation expectations of unrelated central banks. Every $10 added to oil adds approximately 0.3 percentage points to eurozone CPI within two months. That is the difference between the ECB cutting in June and the ECB cutting in September. Globally, what this demonstrates is the degree to which the major central banks are hostage to events outside their control. The Fed, ECB, and Bank of England are all running monetary policy in an environment where a military strike in the Middle East can materially change their rate paths. That dependency — developed economies' monetary policy being held hostage to geopolitical actors — is one of the defining structural features of the current global economy.

rippleEffects.effect: Oil price floor — how long does it hold?
rippleEffects.detail: Geopolitical risk premiums on oil historically persist for the duration of the underlying political uncertainty — not just the initial event. The 2022 Russia/Ukraine premium took 14 months to fully drain. The current Hormuz threat will maintain a floor under oil prices for as long as the Iran situation remains unresolved. OPEC+ spare capacity could theoretically offset a physical closure, but producers are unlikely to deploy it until a closure actually happens.

Sustained oil above $90 feeds into CPI globally within 6–8 weeks. Every major central bank — Fed, ECB, BoE — faces a harder path to cutting rates. The market had been pricing a global cutting cycle beginning mid-2026. An oil shock delays that cycle. The longer it delays, the more growth suffers without rate relief. This is the most significant second-order effect of the Iran strike.

rippleEffects.effect: Japan and South Korea most exposed
rippleEffects.detail: Japan and South Korea import virtually all of their oil. A sustained $94+ oil price materially widens their trade deficits, weakens their currencies, and imports inflation. The Bank of Japan — which had just begun cautiously normalising rates — faces renewed pressure. The Japanese yen is particularly vulnerable: higher oil costs + wider trade deficit + BoJ on hold = JPY weakness. Watch USD/JPY as the leading indicator.

rippleEffects.effect: Strategic oil reserves — a buying opportunity
rippleEffects.detail: China is the world's largest oil importer. In previous oil spikes, China has used geopolitical disruption as cover to accelerate strategic petroleum reserve purchases — buying at what it calculates will be the temporary peak. This behaviour partially offsets the price spike for producers while signalling Chinese strategic intent. Watch Chinese crude import data in the coming weeks for evidence of this pattern repeating.

India imports approximately 85% of its oil needs, predominantly priced in dollars. Higher oil + stronger dollar is a double pressure on India's current account deficit and the rupee. The RBI will face a choice between defending the rupee (keeping rates higher) and supporting growth. India also has a specific geopolitical exposure — it has been increasing Russian oil imports to offset global prices. Any Hormuz closure disrupts the alternative routing India has been developing.

Oil at $94+ feeds directly into eurozone import inflation. Combined with today's ECB hold, this creates a feedback loop: ECB holds because of inflation → EUR weakens → oil costs more in euros → inflation rises → ECB holds longer. European consumers and businesses face the worst of both worlds: elevated borrowing costs and rising energy prices simultaneously. The probability of a June cut falls with every dollar added to oil.

financialSystem.headline: Energy sector profits. Everyone else pays.
financialSystem.body:
Oil above $90 reshuffles the financial system's winners and losers with unusual clarity. Energy trading desks at the major banks — Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan, Morgan Stanley — will have strong quarters. Physical oil trading, derivatives, and commodity financing all generate higher revenues in a volatile, elevated-price environment. The commodity financing teams that arrange credit facilities for oil producers and traders are in high demand.

For the rest of the banking system, the picture darkens. Higher oil means higher inflation means the Fed keeps rates elevated. That delays the rate cuts that would have unlocked a wave of refinancing activity across leveraged loans, mortgages, and corporate bonds. Every quarter of delay costs deal-making revenues across investment banking divisions globally. The M&A advisory pipeline — already thin — stays frozen as buyers and sellers cannot agree on terminal value assumptions when the rate path is uncertain.

The most acute financial system risk is not the banks themselves but the institutions that lent to energy-intensive businesses at low rates. Airlines with heavily financed fleets, logistics companies with floating-rate debt, manufacturers with thin margins — these are the potential stress points. If oil stays above $90 for two quarters, expect the first wave of covenant waivers and restructuring conversations to begin. Credit desks at the major banks are already quietly reviewing their exposures.

resolutionScenario.summary: This is a politically-driven shock, not an economic one. The resolution path runs through diplomacy and military de-escalation, not through central bank decisions or market mechanisms. That makes the timeline longer and less predictable than most financial shocks.

whatNeedsToHappen.trigger: Iran backs down from Hormuz threat within 72 hours
whatNeedsToHappen.effect: Oil partially reverses — likely back to $82–86 range. Risk premium drains but does not fully disappear. Markets treat this as spike-and-fade. Growth equities recover. Rate cut expectations partially restore.

whatNeedsToHappen.trigger: Gulf state diplomatic mediation activates
whatNeedsToHappen.effect: A structured de-escalation pathway opens. Oil falls gradually over 2–4 weeks as the diplomatic track gains credibility. Markets price a resolution 3–6 months out. The risk premium does not disappear immediately but begins draining in an orderly way.

whatNeedsToHappen.trigger: OPEC+ announces emergency production increase
whatNeedsToHappen.effect: Partially offsets the supply fear. Oil falls $8–12 immediately. Does not resolve the geopolitical risk premium but caps the upside. Buys time for diplomacy to work. This is the most likely near-term market relief valve.

resolutionScenario.worstCase: Hormuz closes for 30+ days. Oil moves to $130–150. Every central bank faces the monetary policy dilemma simultaneously. Global growth forecasts are cut sharply. The 1973 oil shock playbook applies — 18+ months of elevated energy prices, aggressive rate hikes, and a global recession. Historical precedent says political oil shocks that reach this stage take years to fully normalise.

globalPicture.summary: The Iran strike is a genuinely global shock. Oil is priced in dollars and flows through straits that serve the whole world. The impact radiates outward from the Middle East through energy prices, inflation, central bank decisions, and ultimately into the real economy of every country that imports oil — which is most of them.

counterintuitive.question: Nothing has actually been disrupted yet — Hormuz is still open — so why did oil prices spike immediately on a threat that might never materialise?
counterintuitive.answer: Because commodity markets trade probability, not reality. If traders waited for actual barrels to stop moving before adjusting prices, they would always be too late — by the time the disruption is confirmed, the opportunity is gone. A credible 20% chance of Hormuz closing justifies a significant price premium today. This is not speculation or overreaction — it is millions of rational actors simultaneously pricing the same risk. The irony is that the spike itself becomes self-fulfilling: higher oil prices create the economic damage the threat implied, even if the strait never closes. Iran gets the economic leverage of a closure without bearing its own costs of one.

counterintuitive.question: The US is now the world's largest oil producer — so why does a threat to a Middle Eastern shipping lane still hit American consumers at the petrol pump?
counterintuitive.answer: Because the US produces in a global market but sells at a global price. Texas shale oil doesn't stay in Texas — it enters the global pool and trades at roughly the world market price. When Brent rises, US domestic prices follow regardless of local production levels. Energy independence means the US earns more from what it produces; it does not mean American consumers are insulated from global price movements. The US is simultaneously an oil economy beneficiary — its shale industry profits rise — and an oil price victim — its consumers pay more. Both are true at the same time.

counterintuitive.question: Iran threatening to close Hormuz would devastate its own oil exports — so why is this considered a credible threat rather than economic suicide?
counterintuitive.answer: Because asymmetric leverage works precisely when the cost to you is high but the cost to your enemy is higher. Iran exports roughly 1.5 million barrels a day through Hormuz — but the global economy moves 20 million barrels a day through it. Iran loses one revenue stream. The US loses its ability to stabilise the global economy, its European and Asian allies face immediate energy crises, and the domestic political pressure on Washington becomes overwhelming within weeks. Iran is cornered enough that self-harm is rational if it buys negotiating leverage or forces de-escalation on better terms. The threat's credibility comes from exactly that desperation — a country with nothing left to lose is the most dangerous kind.

biasCheckpoints.detail: The causal chain models how financial markets price geopolitical risk. It does not model how this event is experienced by the 86 million people living in Iran, or the 2 million seafarers whose livelihoods depend on Hormuz transit. For these populations, the relevant chain runs through sanctions, food import costs, and employment — not through basis points and discount rates.

The countries most severely affected by oil price spikes relative to their economic size are not the ones that appear most prominently in financial analysis. Sub-Saharan African nations, South Asian economies, and parts of Central America will absorb proportionally larger shocks through fuel subsidies and food prices. Their absence from this map reflects data availability, not impact size.

The resolution scenarios here give primary agency to the US, OPEC+, and Gulf mediators. China — which is Iran's largest trading partner, holds significant diplomatic leverage, and has strong incentives to mediate — is absent from the resolution framework. Chinese-brokered de-escalation is a plausible path that does not appear in Western financial analysis. This is a meaningful gap.

impactCards.headline: $90+ on Hormuz closure threat
impactCards.body: 20% of global oil transits Hormuz. Even partial closure threat prices in a lasting risk premium.

impactCards.label: Shipping
impactCards.headline: Tanker day rates spike
impactCards.body: War risk premium drives freight rates. Frontline, Euronav benefit. All importers pay more.

impactCards.label: Gold
impactCards.headline: Safe haven bid above $2,900
impactCards.body: Geopolitical risk premium drives flight to gold. Miners re-rate with high operating leverage.

ecosystemMap.layerExplanation: The Hormuz strait is the single most important chokepoint in global energy trade — 20% of global oil and 25% of global LNG transits it. Even a credible threat of closure reprices global energy markets because insurance and rerouting costs are immediate even without physical blockade.
ecosystemMap.storyPosition: Markets are not pricing a Hormuz closure — they are pricing the probability of one. That distinction matters: risk premium can reverse rapidly on de-escalation, while physical closure cannot.
